perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall exercise such care as the Disclosing Party observes to protect Information; <PAGE> c. to restrict dissemination of Information within its organization to employees having a need to know in connection with the purpose of the disclosure set forth above, and to ensure that such employees are informed of the proprietary and confidential nature thereof and that they agree to and observe the requirements of confidentiality set forth herein; d. not to disclose Information to any other party without the written authorization of Disclosing Party; e. not to duplicate Information without the express written permission of Disclosing Party;
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall endeavor to exercise care to protect Information; <PAGE> c. to make reasonable efforts to restrict dissemination of Information within its organization; d. to try not to disclose Information to any other party without the written authorization of Disclosing Party; e. duplication of Information should be avoided without the express written permission of Disclosing Party;
perturbation.explanation: Replaced 'shall exercise' with 'shall endeavor to exercise', 'restrict dissemination' with 'make reasonable efforts to restrict dissemination', 'not to disclose' with 'try not to disclose', and 'not to duplicate' with 'duplication should be avoided'. These changes introduce ambiguity and weaken the obligations on the Receiving Party.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text uses vague terms like 'endeavor to exercise', 'make reasonable efforts', and 'try not to disclose,' which do not meet the standard of reasonable measures required by the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act to protect trade secrets. The Act requires specific and demonstrable efforts to maintain secrecy, not just vague attempts.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changes introduce ambiguity, weakening the legal obligations on the Receiving Party. The original text used mandatory language ('shall exercise,' 'restrict dissemination,' 'not to disclose,' 'not to duplicate'), while the modified text uses weaker, more discretionary language ('endeavor to exercise,' 'make reasonable efforts,' 'try not to disclose,' 'duplication should be avoided'). This contradicts the requirement under the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act to take reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, as explained in the 'Law Explanation.' Snippet 1, CIV 3426.1, defines 'improper means' as including 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy.' By weakening the obligation to maintain secrecy through ambiguous language, the modified text increases the risk of such a breach, potentially failing to meet the required standard of reasonable measures for trade secret protection. The weakened obligations create a legal contradiction because they undermine the enforceability and effectiveness of the confidentiality provisions.

perturbation.original_text: f. to return or destroy Information which is in written or physical form, together with any and all copies, negatives, or reproductions or derivative works in any media, promptly upon request of Disclosing Party or upon termination or completion of the Agreement. 3. Disclosing Party acknowledges that the Agreement does not apply to Information which: a. is at the time of its disclosure publicly known through no wrongful act of Receiving Party;
perturbation.changed_text: f. to return or destroy Information which is in written or physical form, together with any and all copies, negatives, or reproductions or derivative works in any media, when reasonably practical upon request of Disclosing Party or upon termination or completion of the Agreement. 3. Disclosing Party acknowledges that the Agreement does not apply to Information which: a. is at the time of its disclosure publicly known through no intentional act of Receiving Party;
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'when reasonably practical' introduces ambiguity to the obligation to return or destroy information. Changing 'no wrongful act' to 'no intentional act' narrows the scope of the exception, potentially including information that became public due to negligence.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: By narrowing the exception to 'no intentional act,' the modified text could be interpreted as restricting the Receiving Party's ability to use information that becomes publicly known due to unintentional acts (e.g., negligence) potentially impacting their ability to compete, which is generally prohibited under California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 regarding restraints of trade.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)

perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity and narrows an exception, potentially restraining trade, which contradicts California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. The original text's use of 'no wrongful act' is broader than the changed text's 'no intentional act'. This narrowing could restrict the Receiving Party's ability to use information that becomes publicly known due to unintentional acts (e.g., negligence), potentially impacting their ability to compete. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void', and the modified text introduces a restraint not present in the original, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Because of the trade secret subject matter of Entera's business, Disclosee agrees that it will not solicit the services of any of the employees of Entera during the term of this Agreement and for ninety (90) days thereafter.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Because of the subject matter of Entera's business, Disclosee agrees that it will generally refrain from soliciting the services of any of the employees of Entera during the term of this Agreement and for approximately ninety (90) days thereafter.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'trade secret subject matter' to 'subject matter', 'will not solicit' to 'will generally refrain from soliciting', and 'ninety (90) days' to 'approximately ninety (90) days' significantly weakens the non-solicitation agreement. 'Generally refrain' allows for exceptions, and 'approximately ninety days' introduces uncertainty.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: While California generally prohibits non-compete agreements, narrowly tailored non-solicitation agreements can be enforceable to protect trade secrets. However, the weakened language ('generally refrain,' 'approximately ninety days') makes the agreement overly broad and less likely to be considered a reasonable restriction necessary to protect trade secrets, potentially violating the spirit of California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which favors open competition.

perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguities that weaken the enforceability of the non-solicitation agreement, potentially conflicting with the principle of open competition favored by California law. The original text's 'will not solicit' becomes 'will generally refrain from soliciting,' introducing an allowance for solicitation. 'Ninety (90) days' is changed to 'approximately ninety (90) days,' which creates uncertainty about the duration of the restriction. While Section 16600 allows for some restraints on trade, the softened language makes it less likely to be considered a reasonable restriction to protect trade secrets. As stated in the Law Explanation and supported by the scraped law snippets, California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 favors open competition and voids contracts restraining trade. The changes introduce ambiguity, potentially violating the law.

perturbation.original_text: 9. Each Party acknowledges that the unauthorized disclosure or use by the Receiving Party of the Disclosing Party's proprietary and confidential Information will cause irreparable harm and significant injury, the degree of which may be difficult to ascertain. Accordingly, each Party agrees that the Disclosing Party will have the right to obtain an immediate injunction enjoining any breach of this Agreement, as well as the right to pursue any and all other rights and remedies available at law or in equity for such a breach.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. Each Party acknowledges that the unauthorized disclosure or use by the Receiving Party of the Disclosing Party's proprietary and confidential Information could potentially cause harm. Accordingly, each Party agrees that the Disclosing Party may seek to obtain an injunction enjoining any significant breach of this Agreement, as well as the right to consider pursuing other rights and remedies available at law or in equity for such a breach.
perturbation.explanation: Replaced 'will cause irreparable harm and significant injury' with 'could potentially cause harm', 'immediate injunction' with 'may seek to obtain an injunction', 'any breach' with 'any significant breach', and 'pursue any and all other rights and remedies' with 'consider pursuing other rights and remedies'. These changes weaken the assertion of harm and make the remedies discretionary rather than guaranteed.
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) allows for injunctive relief to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets. By changing the language to 'could potentially cause harm' and 'may seek to obtain an injunction,' the modified text weakens the likelihood of obtaining an injunction, even if misappropriation occurs, which goes against the intent of CUTSA to provide strong protection for trade secrets and allow for prompt legal remedies. The requirement for a 'significant breach' adds another layer of ambiguity, making it harder to enforce the agreement under CUTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.2 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.2. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the protections offered by trade secret law. The original text asserts that unauthorized disclosure 'will cause irreparable harm,' while the modified text states it 'could potentially cause harm.' Additionally, the original text grants the right to 'an immediate injunction,' whereas the modified text states the party 'may seek to obtain an injunction.' These changes introduce ambiguity and make injunctive relief less certain. Snippet 1, from California Code CIV 3426.2(a), states that 'Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined.' The original contract language aligns with the ability to obtain an injunction, but the altered language makes obtaining an injunction less definitive, contradicting the law's provision for injunctive relief in cases of threatened misappropriation by introducing ambiguity and weakening the assurance of remedy.
